statement.markdown:
Given the root of a binary tree, return the vertical order traversal of its nodes' values. (i.e., from left to right, column by column).

If two nodes are in the same column and their rows are different, then the node with the smaller row number comes first.

If two nodes are in the same column and row, then their order does not matter.
statement.inputFormat:
The first line contains a single integer N, the number of nodes in the tree.
The next N lines contain node values and their children, in the format: 'value left_child right_child' where left_child and right_child are the 0-based indices of the left and right child nodes respectively, or -1 if the child doesn't exist.
statement.outputFormat: The output consists of multiple lines, each representing a column in the vertical order traversal from leftmost column to rightmost column. The values in each column are printed on a single line separated by spaces.

constraints.description: The number of nodes in the binary tree.
The values of the nodes in the binary tree.

hints.text: To solve this problem, we need to understand the concept of vertical order traversal in a binary tree. This means we have to traverse the tree column by column from left to right. We should start by thinking about how we can assign a column number to each node in the tree.

We can use a hash map or dictionary to store the nodes at each column index. The key will be the column index, and the value will be a list of node values at that column. To assign the column index, we can use the fact that for every node, its left child will be one column to the left, and its right child will be one column to the right.

To ensure that nodes in the same column are ordered by their row number, we can also keep track of the row number of each node. This can be done by using a pair or tuple as the key in our hash map, where the first element is the column index and the second element is the row number. Alternatively, we can use a separate data structure to keep track of the row numbers.

The actual traversal of the tree can be done using a breadth-first search (BFS) approach, where we use a queue to keep track of the nodes to visit next. For each node, we calculate its column index and row number, and then add it to the corresponding list in our hash map. After visiting all nodes, we can construct the result by iterating over the hash map and adding the node values to the result list.

editorial.title: Vertical Order Traversal of a Binary Tree
editorial.intuition: The problem asks for a vertical order traversal of a binary tree, which means we need to traverse the tree column by column from left to right. The brute force approach would be to perform a level order traversal and keep track of the column number for each node, but this would be slow due to the overhead of sorting the nodes at each level.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force approach would involve using a queue to perform a level order traversal and a dictionary to store the column numbers for each node. However, this approach would have a high time complexity due to the need to sort the nodes at each level, resulting in a time complexity of O(N log N) in the worst case.
editorial.betterSolution: A better approach would be to use a hash map to store the column numbers for each node and a queue to perform a level order traversal. This approach would allow us to avoid the overhead of sorting the nodes at each level.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves using a hash map to store the column numbers for each node and a queue to perform a level order traversal. We use a dictionary to store the column numbers for each node, where the key is the column number and the value is a list of node values in that column. We then perform a level order traversal, and for each node, we add its value to the corresponding column in the dictionary. Finally, we sort the columns and print the node values in each column.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by showing that it correctly handles the column numbers for each node. Since we use a dictionary to store the column numbers for each node, we can ensure that the node values are correctly assigned to their respective columns. Additionally, the use of a queue to perform a level order traversal ensures that the nodes are visited in the correct order.
editorial.dryRun: To test the optimal solution, we can perform a dry run on a sample binary tree and verify that the output matches the expected result.
commonMistakes: Not handling the case where a node has no children
Not correctly updating the column numbers for each node
Not using a dictionary to store the column numbers for each node
Not performing a level order traversal
Not sorting the columns before printing the node values
